5.1.1.intuition: API versioning is like publishing editions of a textbook. Students using the 3rd edition should not have their book suddenly rewritten mid-semester. You release a 4th edition, keep the 3rd available for a while, and eventually stop printing it.

exercises.title: Implement URI Path Versioning in Express
exercises.description: Create an Express app with /api/v1/products and /api/v2/products. V1 returns { products: [...] }. V2 returns { data: [...], meta: { total, page } }. Both should share the same underlying product service.
exercises.hint: Use express.Router() for each version and mount them with app.use('/api/v1', v1Router).

exercises.title: Add Deprecation Headers
exercises.description: Write middleware that adds Deprecation, Sunset, and Link headers to v1 routes. Log a warning every time a deprecated endpoint is called, including the client's user-agent.
exercises.hint: Use res.set() for headers and req.headers['user-agent'] for the client info.

exercises.title: NestJS Built-In Versioning
exercises.description: Set up a NestJS app using VersioningType.URI. Create a UsersController with @Version('1') and @Version('2') decorated methods that return different response shapes.
exercises.hint: Call app.enableVersioning() in main.ts before app.listen().

exercises.title: Build a Version-Aware API Gateway
exercises.description: Create an Express middleware that reads the version from either the URL path, a header, or a query param (in that priority order) and attaches it to the request object. Write tests for all three sources.
exercises.hint: Parse the URL for /v{n}/ pattern first, then check X-API-Version header, then ?version= query param.

problems.problem: Clients break when you add a new version
problems.cause: Default version is not set, so clients without explicit version get routed to the newest (breaking) version.
problems.solution: Always set a default version. Clients without a version header or path should get the oldest supported version, not the newest.

problems.problem: Code duplication across versions
problems.cause: Each version has a full copy of controllers and services.
problems.solution: Share business logic in services. Only the controller/transformer layer should differ between versions. Use a transformation layer to map internal models to version-specific DTOs.

problems.problem: Too many active versions to maintain
problems.cause: No deprecation policy or sunset dates communicated to consumers.
problems.solution: Publish a versioning policy (e.g., support N-1 versions, 12-month sunset window). Use Deprecation and Sunset headers. Monitor usage of old versions and actively communicate timelines.

further_reading.title: API Versioning Has No Right Answer

5.1.2.intuition: HATEOAS is like a choose-your-own-adventure book. Instead of memorizing which page to flip to, the book tells you at the bottom of each page: 'To fight the dragon, turn to page 47. To flee, turn to page 23.' The available choices change based on where you are in the story.

exercises.title: Build a HAL Response Wrapper
exercises.description: Create a reusable HalBuilder class that can wrap any resource with _links and _embedded. Support addLink(rel, href, method?), embed(rel, resources), and build() methods.
exercises.hint: Use the builder pattern: each method returns 'this' for chaining.

exercises.title: State-Driven Order API
exercises.description: Build an Express API for orders with statuses: created, paid, shipped, delivered, cancelled. Each GET /orders/:id response should include only the valid next-action links for the current state.
exercises.hint: Create a state machine map: { created: ['pay', 'cancel'], paid: ['ship', 'refund'], ... } and generate links from it.

exercises.title: HAL Collection with Pagination
exercises.description: Implement a GET /api/articles endpoint that returns a HAL collection with _embedded articles and pagination links (self, first, last, next, prev). Omit next on the last page and prev on the first page.
exercises.hint: Calculate totalPages = Math.ceil(total / perPage) and conditionally include next/prev links.

problems.problem: Clients ignore the _links and hardcode URLs anyway
problems.cause: Developers find it easier to construct URLs themselves than parse _links from responses.
problems.solution: Document clearly that URLs may change. Provide a client SDK that navigates via links. Consider whether full HATEOAS is worth the overhead for your use case.

problems.problem: Circular references in _embedded cause infinite serialization
problems.cause: A post embeds its author, and the author embeds their posts.
problems.solution: Only embed one level deep. Use links (href) for deeper relationships instead of embedding. Define a clear embedding depth policy.

5.1.3.intuition: Offset pagination is like saying 'skip the first 100 people in line and give me the next 20.' If people join or leave the line, you will count wrong. Cursor pagination is like saying 'start from the person wearing the red hat and give me the next 20' -- no matter how the line changes, you always start from the right spot.

exercises.title: Implement All Three Pagination Styles
exercises.description: Build an Express API for a 'products' resource that supports all three pagination styles via a query parameter: ?style=offset, ?style=cursor, ?style=keyset. Each should return the same data but with appropriate navigation metadata.
exercises.hint: Create three separate query builder functions and select based on the style param.

exercises.title: Relay-Style Cursor Pagination
exercises.description: Implement a GraphQL-compatible Relay connection type with edges, nodes, cursors, and pageInfo (hasNextPage, hasPreviousPage, startCursor, endCursor). Support both 'first/after' (forward) and 'last/before' (backward) pagination.
exercises.hint: For backward pagination, reverse the sort order, fetch, then reverse the results back.

exercises.title: Benchmark Offset vs Keyset
exercises.description: Seed a PostgreSQL table with 1 million rows. Write a script that fetches page 1, page 100, page 1000, and page 50000 using both offset and keyset pagination. Measure and compare the response times.
exercises.hint: Use console.time/console.timeEnd and make sure you have an appropriate index for keyset queries.

problems.problem: Duplicate or missing items when using offset pagination
problems.cause: Records are inserted or deleted between page fetches, shifting the offset boundary.
problems.solution: Switch to cursor-based pagination, or if offset is required, add a 'snapshot' timestamp filter: WHERE created_at <= :snapshot_time.

problems.problem: Extremely slow responses on deep pages with offset
problems.cause: OFFSET N requires the database to scan and discard N rows before returning results.
problems.solution: Switch to keyset/cursor pagination. If offset is required, set a maximum page limit (e.g., max 1000 pages) and suggest search/filter instead.

problems.problem: Cursor pagination does not support jumping to a specific page
problems.cause: Cursors are sequential; you can only move forward or backward from a known position.
problems.solution: Accept this trade-off. For UIs that need page jumping, use offset. For infinite scroll or feeds, cursors are superior. You can also provide a 'total count' endpoint separately.

further_reading.title: Use The Index, Luke - Pagination
further_reading.note: Excellent deep dive into keyset pagination performance

5.1.4.intuition: Think of filtering, sorting, and field selection like ordering at a restaurant. Filtering is choosing a section of the menu ('only vegetarian dishes'). Sorting is asking the waiter to list them by price. Sparse fieldsets is saying 'just tell me the name and price, I do not need the full description.'

exercises.title: Build a Reusable Query Parser
exercises.description: Create a QueryParser class that accepts Express req.query and outputs a Prisma-compatible { where, orderBy, select, skip, take } object. Support operator filters (gt, lt, gte, lte, contains, in), multi-field sort with - prefix, and field selection. Write tests for each feature.
exercises.hint: Parse bracket notation with a regex like /^(\w+)\[(\w+)\]$/ and map operators to Prisma equivalents.

exercises.title: Add Query Validation with Zod
exercises.description: Create a Zod schema that validates and transforms query parameters for a /products endpoint. Validate that sort fields are from an allowed list, price filters are numeric, status is a valid enum, and limit does not exceed 100.
exercises.hint: Use z.coerce.number() for numeric string params and z.enum() for allowed values.

exercises.title: NestJS Query Decorator
exercises.description: Create a custom @ApiQuery() parameter decorator for NestJS that parses query params into a typed QueryOptions object. Use it on a controller method like: @Get() findAll(@ApiQuery() query: QueryOptions).
exercises.hint: Use createParamDecorator from @nestjs/common and access ctx.switchToHttp().getRequest().query.

problems.problem: SQL injection through filter parameters
problems.cause: User-supplied filter field names or values are concatenated directly into SQL strings.
problems.solution: Always whitelist allowed filter fields. Use parameterized queries or an ORM. Never interpolate user input into SQL strings.

problems.problem: Sorting by non-indexed columns causes slow queries
problems.cause: The database performs a full table scan to sort results.
problems.solution: Only allow sorting on indexed columns. Create composite indexes for common sort combinations. Monitor slow query logs.

problems.problem: Overly complex filter combinations cause timeouts
problems.cause: Clients send many simultaneous filters that result in complex, unoptimized query plans.
problems.solution: Limit the number of simultaneous filters (e.g., max 5). Use EXPLAIN ANALYZE to identify problematic combinations. Add appropriate composite indexes.

further_reading.title: Google API Design Guide - Standard Fields

5.1.5.intuition: RFC 7807 is like standardized shipping labels. Without a standard, every warehouse puts the address in a different spot and you waste time figuring out where to look. With a standard label, any handler anywhere in the world can immediately find the destination, return address, and tracking number in the same place every time.

exercises.title: Build a ProblemDetails Error Library
exercises.description: Create a reusable npm-style module with a ProblemDetails class and factory functions for common HTTP errors (400, 401, 403, 404, 409, 422, 429, 500). Include proper TypeScript types and a toJSON() method. Write tests to verify the output structure.
exercises.hint: Each factory function should accept context-specific parameters (e.g., notFound takes resource type and id) and return a ProblemDetails instance.

exercises.title: Integrate with Zod Validation
exercises.description: Write Express middleware that catches Zod validation errors and transforms them into RFC 7807 responses with a structured errors array. Each validation issue should map to { field, message, code }.
exercises.hint: Zod errors have an .issues array with path (string[]) and message properties.

exercises.title: NestJS Global Exception Filter
exercises.description: Implement a NestJS exception filter that catches all exceptions (HttpException, ProblemDetails, PrismaClientKnownRequestError, and generic Error) and returns RFC 7807 formatted responses. Register it globally.
exercises.hint: Use @Catch() with no arguments to catch all exceptions. Check instanceof for each error type.

exercises.title: Error Type Documentation Page
exercises.description: Create an endpoint GET /errors/:type that returns HTML documentation for each error type. When a client follows the 'type' URI from an error response, they should see a description, possible causes, and example resolution steps.
exercises.hint: Store error type docs in a Map<string, { description, causes, resolution }> and render them as HTML.

problems.problem: Stack traces leak to production clients
problems.cause: The error handler includes err.stack or err.message from unexpected errors in the response.
problems.solution: Only include detailed error messages in development. In production, return a generic 'An unexpected error occurred' detail and log the actual error server-side.

problems.problem: Validation errors return inconsistent formats
problems.cause: Different validation libraries (Zod, Joi, class-validator) produce different error structures.
problems.solution: Normalize all validation errors in a single error handler into the standard RFC 7807 format with a consistent errors array.

problems.problem: The 'type' URI in error responses returns 404
problems.cause: The error type URIs are defined but no documentation page exists at that URL.
problems.solution: Either host actual documentation at each error type URI, or use 'about:blank' as the type (which is the RFC default for errors where the type URI is just the HTTP status definition).

further_reading.title: RFC 7807 - Problem Details for HTTP APIs
Zalando RESTful API Guidelines - Error Handling

5.2.1.intuition: GraphQL is like a custom sandwich shop where you pick every ingredient from a menu, versus REST which is like a fixed-menu restaurant where you get the chef's predetermined combo meal -- even if you only wanted the salad.

exercises.title: Build a Blog GraphQL API
exercises.description: Create an Apollo Server with types for User, Post, and Comment. Implement Query resolvers for fetching users, posts (with filtering by status), and a single post by ID. Implement Mutation resolvers for creating and updating posts. Use Prisma for the data layer.
exercises.hint: Start with the schema SDL, then implement resolvers one by one. Test queries in Apollo Sandbox at http://localhost:4000/graphql.

exercises.title: Implement Input Validation
exercises.description: Add input validation to mutations using Zod. Validate that post titles are between 5-200 characters, body is at least 50 characters, and tags array has at most 10 items. Return proper GraphQL errors with validation details.
exercises.hint: Parse input with zodSchema.safeParse() in the resolver and throw a GraphQLError with the issues in extensions.

exercises.title: Add Pagination to Queries
exercises.description: Implement Relay-style cursor pagination for the posts query. Return a Connection type with edges (node + cursor), pageInfo (hasNextPage, hasPreviousPage, startCursor, endCursor), and totalCount.
exercises.hint: Define PostConnection, PostEdge, and PageInfo types in SDL. Use base64-encoded IDs as cursors.

problems.problem: N+1 query problem with nested resolvers
problems.cause: Each field-level resolver (e.g., Post.author) makes a separate database query for every parent record.
problems.solution: Use DataLoader to batch and cache database queries. This is covered in depth in lesson 5.2.3.

problems.problem: GraphQL queries can be arbitrarily deep and expensive
problems.cause: Clients can request deeply nested data (user -> posts -> comments -> author -> posts -> ...) causing massive database load.
problems.solution: Implement query depth limiting, query complexity analysis, and field-level cost directives. Apollo Server supports plugins for this.

problems.problem: Cannot use HTTP caching with GraphQL
problems.cause: All GraphQL requests go to the same POST /graphql endpoint, so CDNs and HTTP caches cannot differentiate between queries.
problems.solution: Use Apollo Server's built-in response caching plugin, persisted queries with GET requests, or a CDN that supports GraphQL-aware caching.

5.2.2.intuition: Think of resolvers as a team of specialized workers on an assembly line. The parent resolver hands off its result to child resolvers, who each do their specific job (fetch comments, look up the author, etc.). Context is the shared toolbox everyone on the line has access to -- the wrench (database), the badge scanner (auth), and the parts catalog (DataLoader).

exercises.title: Build a Context with Authentication
exercises.description: Set up Apollo Server with Express and implement a context function that extracts a JWT token from the Authorization header, verifies it, and attaches the user ID and role to the context. Create a 'me' query that returns the current user.
exercises.hint: Use jwt.verify() in the context function. Catch errors silently so unauthenticated requests still work for public queries.

exercises.title: Implement an @auth Directive
exercises.description: Create a custom @auth directive that supports role-based access. Decorate sensitive fields with @auth(requires: ADMIN). Test that unauthenticated users get an error and that regular users cannot access admin-only fields.
exercises.hint: Use mapSchema from @graphql-tools/utils to transform field resolvers. Wrap the original resolver with an auth check.

exercises.title: Create a Logging Plugin
exercises.description: Build an Apollo Server plugin that logs operation name, type (query/mutation), duration, and any errors for every request. Format the output as structured JSON suitable for a log aggregation system.
exercises.hint: Use requestDidStart to capture the start time and return lifecycle hooks (willSendResponse, didEncounterErrors).

exercises.title: Implement a DateTime Custom Scalar
exercises.description: Create a DateTime scalar that serializes JS Date objects to ISO strings, parses ISO string inputs back to Date objects, and validates the format. Register it in your resolver map.
exercises.hint: Implement serialize, parseValue, and parseLiteral methods on a new GraphQLScalarType instance.

problems.problem: Context is undefined or missing fields in resolvers
problems.cause: The context function in expressMiddleware is not returning the expected object, or an async operation inside it fails silently.
problems.solution: Add error logging inside the context function. Make sure it always returns an object, even when authentication fails. Check that you are passing the context function to expressMiddleware, not to the ApolloServer constructor.

problems.problem: Directive transformer does not apply to mutations
problems.cause: The MapperKind used in mapSchema does not cover the MUTATION type.
problems.solution: MapperKind.OBJECT_FIELD applies to all object types including Query and Mutation. Make sure the directive is declared in SDL with 'on FIELD_DEFINITION' and applied to mutation fields.

problems.problem: Resolver changes do not take effect
problems.cause: Apollo Server caches the schema at startup. Hot reloading requires special configuration.
problems.solution: Restart the server after resolver changes. For development, use nodemon or tsx --watch to auto-restart on file changes.

5.2.3.intuition: DataLoader is like a mail carrier who waits until everyone on the street has put their letters in the mailbox, then does one trip to the post office with all the letters -- instead of making a separate trip for each letter. Subscriptions are like subscribing to a newspaper: instead of checking the newsstand every hour, the paper is delivered to your door whenever a new edition is printed.

exercises.title: Implement DataLoaders for a Blog API
exercises.description: Create DataLoaders for: users by ID, posts by author ID, comments by post ID, and likes count by post ID. Integrate them into Apollo Server context. Verify with Prisma query logging that N+1 queries are eliminated.
exercises.hint: The batch function must return results in the exact same order as the input keys. Use a Map to look up results by key.

exercises.title: Build Real-Time Comments with Subscriptions
exercises.description: Add a commentAdded subscription that pushes new comments to clients watching a specific post. Use graphql-ws for the WebSocket transport. Test by opening two browser tabs to Apollo Sandbox.
exercises.hint: Use withFilter to ensure clients only receive comments for the post they are subscribed to.

exercises.title: Add Subscription Authentication
exercises.description: Implement authentication for WebSocket connections. Clients should pass a JWT token in connectionParams. Reject connections with invalid tokens. Include the authenticated user in the subscription context.
exercises.hint: Use the onConnect callback in useServer to validate the token and return false to reject invalid connections.

problems.problem: DataLoader returns results in wrong order
problems.cause: The batch function does not return results in the same order as the input keys.
problems.solution: Create a Map from the batch query results, then map over the input keys to return results in order: keys.map(id => resultMap.get(id) || null).

problems.problem: Subscriptions work in development but not in production
problems.cause: The in-memory PubSub does not work across multiple server instances.
problems.solution: Use graphql-redis-subscriptions with a shared Redis instance. All server instances publish and subscribe through Redis.

problems.problem: DataLoader cache returns stale data after mutations
problems.cause: DataLoader caches results per request, but if a mutation updates a record and the same request queries it again, the cached (old) version is returned.
problems.solution: Clear specific DataLoader keys after mutations: context.loaders.userLoader.clear(userId). Or create fresh loaders per request (which is the recommended pattern).

further_reading.title: Solving the N+1 Problem in GraphQL

5.3.1.intuition: tRPC is like having a direct phone line between two people who speak the same language, versus REST which is like sending letters that need to be translated at both ends. Since both sides speak TypeScript, there is no need for a translation layer (schema, codegen) -- you just talk directly.

exercises.title: Build a tRPC CRUD API
exercises.description: Create a tRPC router with full CRUD operations for a 'todo' resource: list (with cursor pagination), getById, create, update, toggleComplete, and delete. Use Zod for input validation and Prisma for data access.
exercises.hint: Define input schemas with Zod for each procedure. Use z.object() for structured inputs.

exercises.title: Implement Auth Middleware Chain
exercises.description: Create three tRPC middleware: logging (logs duration), authentication (checks JWT), and rate limiting (max 100 requests per minute per user). Chain them together to create a rateLimitedProtectedProcedure.
exercises.hint: Middleware chains with publicProcedure.use(log).use(auth).use(rateLimit). Each returns next() with potentially modified context.

exercises.title: Nested Routers with Shared Context
exercises.description: Create nested routers: appRouter -> admin (adminProcedure base), appRouter -> public (publicProcedure base). Admin routes should include user management and analytics. Test that auth is enforced on admin routes.
exercises.hint: Use adminProcedure as the base for all procedures in the admin router.

problems.problem: TypeScript errors when importing AppRouter type on the client
problems.cause: The client cannot import server code directly; it needs only the type, not the runtime code.
problems.solution: Export only the type: 'export type AppRouter = typeof appRouter'. Use 'import type { AppRouter }' on the client to ensure no runtime code is imported.

problems.problem: Context types are 'any' in procedure handlers
problems.cause: The tRPC instance was not initialized with the context type.
problems.solution: Pass the context type when initializing: initTRPC.context<Context>().create(). This propagates types to all procedures.

problems.problem: Zod validation errors are not descriptive on the client
problems.cause: By default, tRPC wraps Zod errors in a BAD_REQUEST TRPCError without the field-level details.
problems.solution: The Zod errors are included in the error's 'cause' field. On the client, access error.data?.zodError for structured validation errors.

5.3.2.intuition: Think of Zod + tRPC like a typed pipe connecting two rooms. Whatever shape you pour into one end is guaranteed to come out the other end in the exact same shape. If someone tries to pour in something that does not fit, it gets rejected immediately at the entrance rather than leaking through and causing a mess in the other room.

exercises.title: Build a Type-Safe API with Complex Validation
exercises.description: Create a tRPC router for a 'products' resource with Zod validation: create (name 3-200 chars, price positive number with 2 decimal max, category from enum, optional tags array max 5), update (all fields optional), and list (with pagination and filters for price range, category, and search). Set up a client and verify full auto-complete works.
exercises.hint: Use z.number().positive().multipleOf(0.01) for price and z.enum() for category.

exercises.title: Add Output Validation
exercises.description: Add .output() schemas to your procedures that strip sensitive fields (like password, internalNotes) from responses. Verify that the client type does not include the stripped fields.
exercises.hint: Define a separate output schema that omits sensitive fields. The .output() schema both validates and infers the client-side return type.

exercises.title: Implement Request Batching
exercises.description: Set up a tRPC client with httpBatchLink. Write a function that fetches a user profile, their recent posts, and their notification count in a single batched request. Verify with network inspection that only one HTTP request is made.
exercises.hint: Use Promise.all() with multiple trpc.*.query() calls. The httpBatchLink automatically batches them.

problems.problem: Date fields are received as strings on the client
problems.cause: JSON serialization converts Date objects to strings. Without a transformer, tRPC cannot restore them.
problems.solution: Add superjson as a transformer to both server and client: initTRPC.context<Context>().create({ transformer: superjson }) and pass it to the client link.

problems.problem: Client types are stale after server changes
problems.cause: TypeScript's language server has not picked up the latest server types.
problems.solution: Restart the TypeScript language server in your IDE. In VS Code: Cmd+Shift+P -> 'TypeScript: Restart TS Server'. For monorepos, ensure the server package is correctly referenced in tsconfig.json paths.

problems.problem: Zod validation errors are not user-friendly
problems.cause: Default Zod error messages are technical (e.g., 'Expected string, received number').
problems.solution: Add custom error messages to every Zod validator: z.string().min(2, 'Name must be at least 2 characters'). Use z.string({ required_error: 'Name is required' }) for required field messages.

5.3.3.intuition: Integrating tRPC with an existing Express or NestJS app is like adding a fast lane on an existing highway. The old lanes (REST endpoints) still work fine, but the new fast lane (tRPC) offers a smoother, type-checked ride. Over time, you can route more and more traffic to the fast lane.

exercises.title: Add tRPC to an Existing Express App
exercises.description: Take an existing Express REST API and mount tRPC alongside it at /trpc. Migrate one resource (e.g., users CRUD) to tRPC while keeping the old REST endpoints functional. Share the same database client between both.
exercises.hint: Mount tRPC with app.use('/trpc', trpcExpress.createExpressMiddleware(...)). Share the Prisma client via context.

exercises.title: Test tRPC Procedures with createCallerFactory
exercises.description: Write unit tests for a tRPC router using createCallerFactory. Test queries, mutations, error cases (NOT_FOUND, UNAUTHORIZED), and input validation failures. Mock the Prisma client.
exercises.hint: Create a caller with a mock context and call procedures directly: caller.user.getById({ id: '1' }).

exercises.title: NestJS + tRPC Hybrid API
exercises.description: In a NestJS application, add a TrpcModule that mounts tRPC as middleware. Inject NestJS services (UserService, PostService) into the tRPC router. Ensure that NestJS guards work on the tRPC route.
exercises.hint: Implement NestModule.configure() and use consumer.apply(trpcExpress.createExpressMiddleware(...)).forRoutes('/trpc').

problems.problem: NestJS DI services are undefined in tRPC context
problems.cause: The tRPC router is created outside the NestJS DI container, so services are not injected.
problems.solution: Create the tRPC router inside a NestJS provider that receives injected services. Use the TrpcService pattern shown above where the router is built in a method that has access to injected services.

problems.problem: CORS errors when calling tRPC from a different frontend domain
problems.cause: CORS middleware is not configured for the /trpc routes.
problems.solution: Apply cors() middleware before the tRPC middleware: app.use('/trpc', cors({ origin: 'http://localhost:3000' }), trpcMiddleware).

problems.problem: tRPC batch requests timeout with large payloads
problems.cause: Multiple batched requests exceed the Express body size limit or server timeout.
problems.solution: Increase body limit: express.json({ limit: '10mb' }). Configure tRPC maxBatchSize: httpBatchLink({ maxURLLength: 2083 }). Consider splitting very large batches.

5.4.1.intuition: Protocol Buffers are like a precisely engineered shipping container system. Instead of wrapping each item in variable-size gift wrap (JSON), every item goes into a numbered slot in a standardized container. Both the sender and receiver have the same container blueprint (.proto file), so there is never confusion about what goes where.

exercises.title: Design a Proto Schema for an E-Commerce Service
exercises.description: Write a .proto file for an e-commerce service with messages for Product (id, name, description, price, category, stock), Order (id, user_id, items, total, status), and OrderItem (product_id, quantity, unit_price). Define a ProductService and OrderService with CRUD operations.
exercises.hint: Use nested messages for OrderItem inside Order. Use enums for OrderStatus and ProductCategory. Remember to start enum values at 0 with UNSPECIFIED.

exercises.title: Practice Schema Evolution
exercises.description: Start with a simple User message (id, name, email). Evolve it to add phone, address (nested message), and roles (repeated enum). Then remove the 'name' field and split it into first_name and last_name. Use reserved correctly throughout.
exercises.hint: When removing a field, add both the field number and field name to reserved statements.

exercises.title: Generate TypeScript Types from Proto
exercises.description: Set up ts-proto code generation for your .proto files. Write a script that generates TypeScript interfaces and gRPC service definitions. Verify the generated types match your expectations.
exercises.hint: Use protoc with --ts_proto_out and --ts_proto_opt=outputServices=grpc-js for gRPC-compatible output.

problems.problem: Field number reuse causes data corruption
problems.cause: A deleted field's number was reused for a new field with a different type. Old messages are deserialized incorrectly.
problems.solution: Always use 'reserved' for deleted field numbers. Never reuse a field number, even if the old field was removed long ago.

problems.problem: Proto file imports fail with 'file not found'
problems.cause: The protoc compiler cannot find imported .proto files (like google/protobuf/timestamp.proto).
problems.solution: Use --proto_path (-I) to specify include directories. For Google well-known types, install them: npm install google-proto-files, then add the include path.

problems.problem: Enum values collide across messages
problems.cause: Proto3 enums are scoped to the package, not the message. Two enums in the same package with the same value name conflict.
problems.solution: Prefix enum values with the enum name: POST_STATUS_DRAFT, USER_ROLE_ADMIN. This is the protobuf convention.

further_reading.title: Google API Design Guide
further_reading.note: Google's guide for designing proto-based APIs

5.4.2.intuition: gRPC's four communication patterns are like different phone call types. Unary is a regular call (ask a question, get an answer). Server streaming is like calling a radio station that plays songs continuously after you request a genre. Client streaming is like dictating a long message to voicemail. Bidirectional streaming is a normal conversation where both sides talk freely.

exercises.title: Build a Full Unary gRPC Service
exercises.description: Implement a UserService with gRPC unary RPCs: GetUser, CreateUser, UpdateUser, DeleteUser, ListUsers. Use @grpc/grpc-js with proto-loader. Include metadata-based authentication. Write both server and client code.
exercises.hint: Define request/response messages in a .proto file. Load with protoLoader.loadSync. Implement each RPC as a method in the service object.

exercises.title: Implement Server Streaming for Live Updates
exercises.description: Create a gRPC server streaming RPC that watches a database table for changes and pushes new/updated records to connected clients. Simulate changes with setInterval. Handle client cancellation gracefully.
exercises.hint: Use call.write() to send each update and call.on('cancelled') to detect disconnection. Clean up intervals on cancel.

exercises.title: Build a Chat Service with Bidirectional Streaming
exercises.description: Create a Chat gRPC service where multiple clients can send and receive messages in real-time. Maintain a list of active streams and broadcast messages to all connected clients.
exercises.hint: Store active ServerDuplexStream instances in an array. When one client sends a message, iterate over all streams and write the message to each.

problems.problem: gRPC server silently does nothing when started
problems.cause: server.bindAsync is called but the callback error is not checked, or the port is already in use.
problems.solution: Always check the error parameter in the bindAsync callback. Log the port on success. Check for EADDRINUSE errors.

problems.problem: Client receives UNAVAILABLE error
problems.cause: The server is not running, the port is wrong, or there is a network issue.
problems.solution: Verify the server is running on the expected port. Use grpc.credentials.createInsecure() for development. Check firewalls and DNS resolution.

problems.problem: Streaming calls hang indefinitely
problems.cause: The server never calls call.end() for server streams, or the client never calls stream.end() for client streams.
problems.solution: Always call end() when streaming is complete. Set deadlines on the client side. Use call.on('cancelled') on the server to detect and clean up abandoned streams.

5.4.3.body:
# gRPC vs REST vs tRPC: When to Use Which

Choosing the right API paradigm is a critical architectural decision. Each has distinct strengths and trade-offs. This lesson provides a framework for making that choice.

## Feature Comparison

```
+-------------------+----------+-----------+----------+-----------+
| Feature           |  REST    |  GraphQL  |  tRPC    |   gRPC    |
+-------------------+----------+-----------+----------+-----------+
| Protocol          | HTTP/1.1 | HTTP/1.1  | HTTP/1.1 | HTTP/2    |
| Data Format       | JSON     | JSON      | JSON     | Protobuf  |
| Type Safety       | Manual   | Schema    | Auto     | Generated |
| Code Generation   | Optional | Optional  | None     | Required  |
| Browser Support   | Native   | Native    | Native   | Limited*  |
| Streaming         | Limited  | Subscript.| Limited  | Full      |
| Performance       | Good     | Good      | Good     | Excellent |
| Caching           | HTTP     | Complex   | Manual   | Manual    |
| Learning Curve    | Low      | Medium    | Low      | High      |
| Language Agnostic | Yes      | Yes       | No (TS)  | Yes       |
+-------------------+----------+-----------+----------+-----------+
* gRPC-Web or Connect protocol for browser support
```

## When to Use REST

REST is the best choice when:

1. **Public APIs** consumed by third-party developers
2. **Simple CRUD operations** on resources
3. **Browser-based consumption** without libraries
4. **HTTP caching** is important (CDN, browser cache)
5. **Team is familiar** with REST conventions
6. **Hypermedia-driven** workflows (HATEOAS)

```typescript
// REST shines for straightforward resource operations
// GET /api/products/123
// PUT /api/products/123
// DELETE /api/products/123
// GET /api/products?category=electronics&sort=-price

app.get('/api/products/:id', async (req, res) => {
  const product = await productService.findById(req.params.id);
  // HTTP caching works out of the box
  res.set('Cache-Control', 'public, max-age=300');
  res.json(product);
});
```

**Strengths:**
- Universal understanding
- Built-in caching via HTTP headers
- Easy to debug (curl, browser, Postman)
- Mature tooling and documentation (OpenAPI/Swagger)

**Weaknesses:**
- Over-fetching / under-fetching
- No built-in type safety
- Multiple round trips for complex data

## When to Use GraphQL

GraphQL excels when:

1. **Multiple frontends** need different data shapes (mobile vs web)
2. **Complex, nested data** with relationships
3. **Reducing round trips** is critical (mobile apps)
4. **Rapid frontend iteration** without backend changes
5. **API aggregation** across multiple microservices

```graphql
# Mobile app: minimal data
query {
  user(id: "1") {
    name
    avatarUrl
  }
}

# Web dashboard: full data, one request
query {
  user(id: "1") {
    name
    email
    posts {
      title
      comments { text author { name } }
    }
    notifications(unreadOnly: true) { message }
  }
}
```

**Strengths:**
- Client controls response shape
- Single endpoint, single request
- Strong schema with introspection
- Great developer experience (Apollo Studio, GraphiQL)

**Weaknesses:**
- N+1 query problem (needs DataLoader)
- No HTTP caching by default
- Query complexity attacks
- Steeper learning curve

## When to Use tRPC

tRPC is ideal when:

1. **Full-stack TypeScript** (you control both ends)
2. **Internal APIs** (not consumed by third parties)
3. **Rapid development** with auto-complete and type checking
4. **Monorepos** where client and server share types
5. **Small to medium teams** moving fast

```typescript
// tRPC: instant type safety, zero overhead
const user = await trpc.user.getById.query({ id: '1' });
user.name;  // TypeScript knows this is string
user.foo;   // Compile error! Property 'foo' does not exist
```

**Strengths:**
- Zero-cost type safety
- No code generation or schemas to maintain
- Instant auto-complete on the client
- Easy to set up and learn
- Request batching built-in

**Weaknesses:**
- TypeScript only (no other language clients)
- Not suitable for public APIs
- No standard like OpenAPI for documentation
- Tightly couples frontend and backend

## When to Use gRPC

gRPC excels when:

1. **Microservice communication** (service-to-service)
2. **High performance** is critical (binary protocol, HTTP/2)
3. **Streaming** requirements (real-time data, uploads)
4. **Polyglot services** (different languages per service)
5. **Strong contracts** with backward compatibility

```protobuf
// gRPC: binary, fast, multi-language
service OrderService {
  rpc CreateOrder(CreateOrderRequest) returns (Order);
  rpc StreamOrderUpdates(OrderFilter) returns (stream OrderUpdate);
}
```

**Strengths:**
- 3-10x smaller payloads (protobuf)
- Native streaming (all four patterns)
- HTTP/2 multiplexing
- Excellent for service-to-service
- Strong backward compatibility via field numbers

**Weaknesses:**
- Not browser-native (needs gRPC-Web)
- Binary format is hard to debug
- Requires proto compilation toolchain
- Higher learning curve
- Not human-readable

## Decision Framework

```
Are you building a public API?
  YES -> REST (with OpenAPI documentation)
  NO  ->
    Is it service-to-service?
      YES ->
        Need streaming or high performance?
          YES -> gRPC
          NO  -> REST or gRPC (team preference)
      NO  ->
        Is everything TypeScript?
          YES -> tRPC
          NO  ->
            Multiple frontends with different data needs?
              YES -> GraphQL
              NO  -> REST
```

## Hybrid Architectures

In practice, many systems use multiple paradigms:

```
+---------------------------------------------------+
|                                                   |
|  [Mobile App] --GraphQL--> [API Gateway]          |
|  [Web App]    --tRPC-----> [BFF Service]          |
|  [Partner API] --REST----> [Public API]           |
|                                                   |
|       Internal microservices communicate          |
|              via gRPC                             |
|                                                   |
|  [User Service] <--gRPC--> [Order Service]        |
|  [Order Service] <-gRPC--> [Payment Service]      |
|  [Notification Service] <-gRPC--> [Email Service] |
|                                                   |
+---------------------------------------------------+
```

```typescript
// NestJS hybrid: REST + gRPC
// REST for external clients
@Controller('api/products')
export class ProductsController {
  @Get(':id')
  findOne(@Param('id') id: string) {
    return this.productsService.findById(id);
  }
}

// gRPC for internal service communication
@GrpcMethod('ProductService', 'GetProduct')
findOneGrpc(data: { id: string }) {
  return this.productsService.findById(data.id);
}
```

## Performance Benchmarks (Approximate)

```
Operation: Fetch 100 user records

+----------+--------+----------+-----------+
| Metric   | REST   | GraphQL  | gRPC      |
+----------+--------+----------+-----------+
| Payload  | 15 KB  | 8 KB*    | 4 KB      |
| Latency  | 12 ms  | 14 ms    | 5 ms      |
| Requests | 1      | 1        | 1         |
+----------+--------+----------+-----------+
* GraphQL smaller because client requests only needed fields

Note: These are illustrative. Actual numbers depend on
data complexity, network conditions, and implementation.
```

## Common Anti-Patterns

1. **Using gRPC for browser-facing APIs** without gRPC-Web
2. **Using GraphQL for simple CRUD** with no relationships
3. **Using tRPC for public APIs** that need multiple language clients
4. **Using REST for real-time streaming** (use WebSocket or gRPC instead)
5. **Choosing based on hype** instead of actual requirements
5.4.3.intuition: Choosing between REST, GraphQL, tRPC, and gRPC is like choosing between a bicycle, car, motorcycle, and truck. A bicycle (REST) works everywhere and everyone knows how to ride one. A car (GraphQL) is comfortable and flexible for passengers (clients) who want control. A motorcycle (tRPC) is the fastest for a solo TypeScript rider. A truck (gRPC) carries the heaviest loads between warehouses (services) with maximum efficiency.

exercises.title: Architecture Decision Document
exercises.description: You are designing a system with: a public mobile app, an internal admin dashboard, and 5 backend microservices. Write an architecture decision document that recommends which API paradigm to use for each communication path (mobile-to-backend, admin-to-backend, service-to-service) with justification.
exercises.hint: Consider GraphQL or REST for mobile, tRPC for admin dashboard (if TypeScript), and gRPC for service-to-service. Justify each based on the criteria in this lesson.

exercises.title: Build the Same API in REST, tRPC, and gRPC
exercises.description: Implement a simple 'todo' CRUD API (create, read, update, delete, list) using all three paradigms. Compare the code complexity, type safety, and developer experience of each.
exercises.hint: Start with the REST version as a baseline. Note which paradigm requires the most boilerplate and which provides the best auto-complete.

exercises.title: Performance Comparison
exercises.description: Build a benchmark that sends 1000 sequential requests for the same data using REST (JSON), GraphQL (JSON), and gRPC (protobuf). Measure average latency, payload size, and throughput.
exercises.hint: Use console.time for timing. Use JSON.stringify().length for JSON payload size. For gRPC, inspect the serialized buffer length.

problems.problem: Team chose gRPC for everything including the frontend
problems.cause: gRPC was selected based on performance benchmarks without considering browser compatibility.
problems.solution: Use gRPC for service-to-service communication and REST/GraphQL/tRPC for browser-facing APIs. If you must use gRPC from browsers, use gRPC-Web or the Connect protocol.

problems.problem: GraphQL API is slower than the REST API it replaced
problems.cause: N+1 queries, lack of DataLoader, and no response caching.
problems.solution: Implement DataLoader for all relationship resolvers. Add query depth limiting. Use Apollo response cache or persisted queries.

problems.problem: tRPC types are not available to non-TypeScript consumers
problems.cause: tRPC's type inference only works within the TypeScript ecosystem.
problems.solution: For non-TypeScript clients, generate an OpenAPI spec from your tRPC routes using trpc-openapi, or provide a separate REST API for external consumers.

further_reading.title: trpc-openapi - OpenAPI from tRPC
further_reading.note: Generate REST endpoints from tRPC routers

further_reading.title: Connect Protocol - gRPC for the Web

5.5.1.intuition: OpenAPI documentation is like the blueprint for a building. Just as a builder needs the blueprint to understand the layout before constructing anything, API consumers need the OpenAPI spec to understand the available endpoints, data shapes, and rules before writing any code. And just like blueprints can be used to auto-generate material lists, OpenAPI specs can auto-generate client code.

exercises.title: Document a NestJS API with @nestjs/swagger
exercises.description: Take an existing NestJS REST API and add full Swagger documentation. Decorate all DTOs with @ApiProperty, all endpoints with @ApiOperation and @ApiResponse, add authentication documentation with @ApiBearerAuth. Ensure the generated docs at /docs are complete and interactive.
exercises.hint: Use the Swagger CLI plugin in nest-cli.json to auto-generate @ApiProperty from class-validator decorators.

exercises.title: Document an Express API with swagger-jsdoc
exercises.description: Add OpenAPI documentation to an existing Express API using swagger-jsdoc. Write JSDoc comments for at least 5 endpoints covering GET (with query params), POST (with request body), PUT, and DELETE. Include component schemas for all data types.
exercises.hint: Define reusable schemas in components/schemas and reference them with $ref in endpoint annotations.

exercises.title: Generate a TypeScript Client from OpenAPI
exercises.description: Take the OpenAPI spec from your documented API and generate a TypeScript client using openapi-typescript-codegen. Write a small script that uses the generated client to perform CRUD operations.
exercises.hint: Serve the OpenAPI JSON at /docs.json, then point the generator at that URL.

problems.problem: Swagger UI shows incorrect request/response types
problems.cause: DTO decorators are missing or incorrect. NestJS cannot infer types without @ApiProperty decorators.
problems.solution: Add @ApiProperty to every field in your DTOs. Use the NestJS Swagger CLI plugin to auto-generate decorators from class-validator metadata.

problems.problem: Authentication endpoints not showing in Swagger UI
problems.cause: The security scheme is not configured in DocumentBuilder, or endpoints are missing @ApiBearerAuth().
problems.solution: Add .addBearerAuth() to DocumentBuilder and decorate protected endpoints with @ApiBearerAuth(). Users can then click 'Authorize' in Swagger UI.

problems.problem: swagger-jsdoc generates an empty spec
problems.cause: The 'apis' path pattern does not match your route files, or JSDoc comments have syntax errors.
problems.solution: Verify the glob pattern matches your files. Use absolute paths if needed. Validate your JSDoc YAML syntax -- indentation errors are the most common cause.

further_reading.title: OpenAPI Generator
further_reading.note: Generate clients in 50+ languages from an OpenAPI spec

5.5.2.intuition: Contract testing is like two people agreeing on a handshake before a meeting. Before they meet in person (integration), each practices the handshake independently to make sure they will not fumble it. The written handshake description (the contract) ensures both sides know exactly what to expect, even though they practice separately.

exercises.title: Write Consumer-Driven Pact Tests
exercises.description: Create Pact consumer tests for an API client that interacts with a User Service. Define at least 4 interactions: get user, create user, update user, and handle 404. Use Pact matchers (like, eachLike, uuid) for flexible matching.
exercises.hint: Install @pact-foundation/pact. Each test defines an interaction with .given().uponReceiving().withRequest().willRespondWith() then runs in provider.executeTest().

exercises.title: Verify a Provider Against Pacts
exercises.description: Set up provider verification tests that validate your Express API against consumer pacts. Implement stateHandlers to set up test data for each provider state defined in the consumer tests.
exercises.hint: Use the Verifier class with stateHandlers that seed/clean the database for each 'given' state.

exercises.title: Schema Validation Test Suite
exercises.description: Build an automated test suite that validates all API responses against the OpenAPI spec. For each endpoint, make a request and validate the response body against the corresponding schema using Ajv.
exercises.hint: Load the OpenAPI spec, extract schemas for each path/method/status combination, and compile Ajv validators.

problems.problem: Pact verification fails because provider states are not set up
problems.cause: The provider verification test does not implement stateHandlers matching the consumer's .given() strings.
problems.solution: Implement stateHandlers for every provider state string used in consumer tests. The strings must match exactly.

problems.problem: Contract tests pass but integration still fails
problems.cause: Contract tests verify structure, not business logic. The response shape matches but the data is wrong.
problems.solution: Contract tests complement (not replace) integration tests. Use contract tests for structure and integration tests for behavior.

problems.problem: OpenAPI schema validation misses new required fields
problems.cause: The API added a new required field but the schema was not updated.
problems.solution: Generate the OpenAPI spec from code (not manually) using @nestjs/swagger or swagger-jsdoc. This ensures the spec always matches the implementation.

5.6.1.intuition: The Repository Pattern is like a librarian. You never go into the back room to search the shelves yourself (database). Instead, you ask the librarian (repository) to 'find me a book by this author' or 'add this new book to the collection.' If the library reorganizes its shelves (switches databases), your experience at the front desk stays exactly the same.

exercises.title: Implement a Generic Base Repository
exercises.description: Create a generic BaseRepository<T> interface and PrismaBaseRepository<T> implementation that provides findById, findAll, create, update, delete, and count for any Prisma model. Then extend it for a specific UserRepository with custom methods like findByEmail.
exercises.hint: Use generics for the entity type. The base class can accept the Prisma model delegate in its constructor.

exercises.title: Test Service with In-Memory Repository
exercises.description: Build an OrderService with business logic for creating, paying, shipping, and cancelling orders. Write an InMemoryOrderRepository and comprehensive tests for the service using it. No database required.
exercises.hint: The in-memory repository stores orders in an array. The service tests should verify state transitions and business rules.

exercises.title: Swap Implementations at Runtime
exercises.description: Create a NestJS module where the repository implementation can be swapped via configuration: PrismaPostRepository for production, InMemoryPostRepository for testing. Use NestJS custom providers with useFactory.
exercises.hint: Use ConfigService to read the implementation type and return the appropriate repository from useFactory.

problems.problem: Repository interface leaks ORM-specific types
problems.cause: The interface uses Prisma-specific types (like PrismaPromise or Prisma.PostWhereInput) instead of plain domain types.
problems.solution: Define domain types (Post, CreatePostData, etc.) that are ORM-independent. The repository interface should only use these types. Map between Prisma types and domain types inside the implementation.

problems.problem: Repository becomes a 'god object' with too many methods
problems.cause: Every data access need is added as a new method on the repository.
problems.solution: Use a specification/criteria pattern for complex queries. Keep repository methods for common operations and use a query builder for ad-hoc queries.

problems.problem: Transactions across repositories are difficult
problems.cause: Each repository has its own database client instance.
problems.solution: Pass a transaction client through context or use a Unit of Work pattern that coordinates transactions across repositories.

further_reading.title: Prisma Data Mapper vs Active Record

5.6.2.intuition: Think of a restaurant. The waiter (controller) takes your order and brings food, but does not cook. The chef (service) coordinates the cooking process, decides when to plate, and orchestrates the kitchen. The recipe (domain model) contains the cooking rules -- you cannot serve raw chicken, you must season before grilling. Each has a clear role, and swapping out the waiter does not change the recipe.

exercises.title: Build a Rich Domain Model for an Order System
exercises.description: Create an OrderEntity with states: CREATED, PAID, SHIPPED, DELIVERED, CANCELLED, REFUNDED. Implement methods for pay(), ship(), deliver(), cancel(), and refund() with proper state transition validation. Write tests for all valid and invalid transitions.
exercises.hint: Use a state machine approach: each method checks the current state and only transitions if valid. Throw DomainError for invalid transitions.

exercises.title: Implement a Service Layer with Side Effects
exercises.description: Build a PostService that uses a PostRepository (interface), EventBus, and NotificationService. Implement createPost, publishPost, and deletePost with proper authorization, domain validation, and event emission. Write tests using in-memory implementations.
exercises.hint: Inject all dependencies as interfaces. The EventBus can be a simple in-memory implementation for testing.

exercises.title: Refactor from Anemic to Rich Model
exercises.description: Take a service with all business logic in service methods operating on plain data objects. Refactor it to use a rich domain model where entities encapsulate their own validation and state transitions. Keep the service for orchestration only.
exercises.hint: Move each validation check from the service into the corresponding entity method. The service should call entity methods, not implement business rules.

problems.problem: Service becomes a 'god class' with hundreds of methods
problems.cause: All business logic for a domain area is placed in a single service.
problems.solution: Split into focused services: PostQueryService, PostCommandService, PostPublishingService. Use CQRS to separate read and write operations.

problems.problem: Circular dependency between services
problems.cause: ServiceA depends on ServiceB and ServiceB depends on ServiceA.
problems.solution: Extract the shared logic into a third service. Use events for loose coupling: instead of ServiceA calling ServiceB, ServiceA emits an event that ServiceB listens to.

problems.problem: Domain entities have no access to repositories
problems.cause: By design, domain entities should not depend on infrastructure. But some domain logic needs data from other entities.
problems.solution: The service layer is responsible for loading dependencies. Pass all needed data into domain methods as parameters rather than having the entity fetch data itself.

5.6.3.intuition: CQRS is like separating a library's 'check-out desk' from the 'catalog search computer.' The search system can be optimized with indexes and caches without affecting how books are checked in/out. Event Sourcing is like keeping a ledger instead of just a bank balance -- you can always recalculate the balance from the history, and you have a complete record of every transaction.

exercises.title: Implement CQRS for a Blog
exercises.description: Separate your blog API into command handlers (CreatePost, PublishPost, AddComment) and query handlers (GetPostDetail, ListPosts, GetUserProfile). Use a simple event bus to sync the write model to a denormalized read model.
exercises.hint: The write side uses normalized tables. The read side has a 'post_views' table with pre-joined author name, comment count, etc.

exercises.title: Build a Simple Event Store
exercises.description: Create an event store backed by PostgreSQL with a table: (stream_id, version, event_type, data, timestamp). Implement append (with optimistic concurrency), getEvents, and getEventsSince. Build an OrderAggregate that rebuilds from events.
exercises.hint: For optimistic concurrency, include a version check in the INSERT: WHERE version = expected_version.

exercises.title: NestJS CQRS Module
exercises.description: Use @nestjs/cqrs to implement commands, queries, and events for a task management system. Create commands for creating and completing tasks, queries for listing and filtering, and events that update a read model with task statistics.
exercises.hint: Install @nestjs/cqrs and register CqrsModule. Use CommandBus and QueryBus in controllers.

problems.problem: Read model is out of sync with write model
problems.cause: Events are lost or event handlers fail, leaving the read model stale.
problems.solution: Use reliable event delivery (message queue with acknowledgment). Implement a 'rebuild' mechanism that replays all events to reconstruct the read model. Add monitoring for event processing lag.

problems.problem: Event sourced aggregate has too many events, slow to load
problems.cause: Long-lived aggregates accumulate thousands of events that must be replayed on every load.
problems.solution: Implement snapshots: periodically save the current aggregate state. On load, start from the latest snapshot and replay only events since then.

problems.problem: CQRS adds unnecessary complexity to a simple CRUD app
problems.cause: CQRS was applied where a simple service + repository would suffice.
problems.solution: Only use CQRS when you have different read/write scaling needs or complex read models. Start simple and refactor to CQRS when the pain justifies the complexity.

further_reading.title: Greg Young - CQRS and Event Sourcing

5.7.1.intuition: Hexagonal Architecture is like an electrical appliance with standardized plugs (ports). Your toaster's heating logic (domain) does not care whether electricity comes from a wall socket, a generator, or a battery (adapters). The plug shape (port interface) is the contract. You can test the toaster with a test power supply, and deploy it in any country by swapping the adapter.

exercises.title: Refactor to Hexagonal Architecture
exercises.description: Take an existing Express API where controllers directly call Prisma and refactor it to hexagonal architecture. Create domain entities, define input/output ports, implement use cases, and create adapters. Verify the domain layer has zero external dependencies.
exercises.hint: Start from the inside out: define domain entities first, then ports, then use cases, then adapters. The domain folder should have zero imports from node_modules.

exercises.title: Multiple Driving Adapters
exercises.description: Create a single set of use cases that can be driven by both an Express REST controller and a GraphQL resolver. Both adapters should use the same CreatePostPort interface.
exercises.hint: Define the input port once. Create a REST controller adapter and a GraphQL resolver adapter that both call the same use case implementation.

exercises.title: Swap Driven Adapters
exercises.description: Implement the NotificationPort with three adapters: EmailNotificationAdapter, SlackNotificationAdapter, and ConsoleNotificationAdapter (for development). Configure which one is used based on environment variables.
exercises.hint: Create a factory function that reads process.env.NOTIFICATION_ADAPTER and returns the appropriate implementation.

problems.problem: Too many files and interfaces for a simple app
problems.cause: Hexagonal architecture was applied to a small CRUD application that does not benefit from the abstraction.
problems.solution: Use hexagonal architecture for complex domains with multiple adapters or changing requirements. For simple CRUD, a simpler layered architecture (controller -> service -> repository) is fine.

problems.problem: Domain entities import from Prisma or NestJS
problems.cause: Developers accidentally used @nestjs/common decorators or Prisma types in domain entities.
problems.solution: Enforce the dependency rule with linting rules or TypeScript project references that prevent domain from importing infrastructure. The domain folder should have zero imports from node_modules except standard lib.

problems.problem: Mapping between domain and persistence models is tedious
problems.cause: Every repository method needs to convert between Prisma models and domain entities.
problems.solution: Create mapper classes (PostMapper.toDomain(), PostMapper.toPersistence()) to centralize the conversion. Accept the mapping overhead as the cost of decoupling.

5.7.2.intuition: Dependency Inversion is like hiring through a job description instead of calling a specific person. Your company (high-level module) needs 'someone who can handle accounting' (abstraction/interface), not 'specifically Jane from ABC Corp' (concrete implementation). This way, you can hire anyone who meets the job requirements, and you can easily swap them out without restructuring the company.

exercises.title: Refactor Direct Dependencies to Interfaces
exercises.description: Take a service that directly imports PrismaClient, nodemailer, and stripe. Refactor it to depend on interfaces instead. Create production implementations and test fakes for each.
exercises.hint: Extract an interface for each dependency. Move the constructor to accept interfaces. Create concrete classes that implement each interface.

exercises.title: Build a Pluggable Notification System
exercises.description: Create an INotificationService interface with send(userId, message) method. Implement: EmailNotification, SMSNotification, PushNotification, and SlackNotification adapters. Write a service that uses the interface and tests that work with any adapter.
exercises.hint: Each adapter implements the same interface. The service does not know which adapter it uses.

exercises.title: NestJS Multi-Provider Module
exercises.description: Create a NestJS module that configures different adapter implementations based on environment (development, staging, production). In dev, use in-memory repositories and console email. In production, use Prisma and SendGrid.
exercises.hint: Use useFactory providers that read ConfigService to decide which implementation to instantiate.

problems.problem: Too many interfaces for simple operations
problems.cause: Every class has an extracted interface, even when there is only one implementation and no testing benefit.
problems.solution: Extract interfaces only when you have multiple implementations or need to mock in tests. Not every class needs an interface.

problems.problem: Constructor parameter lists become very long
problems.cause: A service depends on many abstractions, leading to 6+ constructor parameters.
problems.solution: This often indicates the service has too many responsibilities. Split it into smaller, focused services. Alternatively, group related dependencies into a single context object.

problems.problem: Wiring dependencies manually is tedious
problems.cause: Without a DI framework, you manually construct and inject every dependency in the composition root.
problems.solution: Use a DI container (NestJS, tsyringe, or inversify) for automatic resolution. Or accept manual wiring as a trade-off for simplicity and transparency.

5.7.3.body:
# Structuring a Large Node.js Backend Project

As Node.js backends grow beyond a few endpoints, ad-hoc file organization becomes unmanageable. This lesson covers proven project structures for scaling from startup prototype to enterprise application.

## Common Anti-Patterns

```
BAD: Technical grouping (scales poorly)
src/
  controllers/
    userController.ts
    postController.ts
    commentController.ts
    orderController.ts
    ... 50 more controllers
  services/
    userService.ts
    postService.ts
    ... 50 more services
  models/
    user.ts
    post.ts
    ... 50 more models
  routes/
    userRoutes.ts
    ...
```

This structure forces you to jump between many folders to work on a single feature.

## Feature-Based Structure (Recommended)

```
src/
  modules/                   # Each feature is self-contained
    user/
      user.controller.ts
      user.service.ts
      user.repository.ts
      user.entity.ts
      user.dto.ts
      user.module.ts          # NestJS module
      __tests__/
        user.service.test.ts
        user.controller.test.ts
        user.e2e.test.ts
    post/
      post.controller.ts
      post.service.ts
      post.repository.ts
      post.entity.ts
      post.dto.ts
      post.module.ts
      __tests__/
    order/
      order.controller.ts
      order.service.ts
      order.repository.ts
      order.entity.ts
      order.saga.ts           # Complex workflow
      order.dto.ts
      order.module.ts
      __tests__/
    auth/
      auth.controller.ts
      auth.service.ts
      auth.guard.ts
      strategies/
        jwt.strategy.ts
        local.strategy.ts
      auth.module.ts
      __tests__/

  shared/                    # Cross-cutting concerns
    database/
      prisma.service.ts
      prisma.module.ts
    middleware/
      logging.middleware.ts
      rate-limit.middleware.ts
    guards/
      roles.guard.ts
    interceptors/
      transform.interceptor.ts
    filters/
      http-exception.filter.ts
    pipes/
      validation.pipe.ts
    decorators/
      current-user.decorator.ts
    utils/
      pagination.ts
      hashing.ts
      date.ts
    types/
      express.d.ts
      common.ts
    errors/
      app.errors.ts

  config/                    # Configuration
    app.config.ts
    database.config.ts
    auth.config.ts
    cache.config.ts

  app.module.ts              # Root module
  main.ts                    # Entry point
```

## Hexagonal/Clean Architecture Structure

For larger, more complex applications:

```
src/
  domain/                    # Business rules (no dependencies)
    entities/
      user.entity.ts
      post.entity.ts
      order.entity.ts
    value-objects/
      email.vo.ts
      money.vo.ts
      address.vo.ts
    events/
      post-created.event.ts
      order-placed.event.ts
    errors/
      domain.error.ts

  application/               # Use cases
    user/
      commands/
        create-user.command.ts
        create-user.handler.ts
      queries/
        get-user.query.ts
        get-user.handler.ts
      ports/
        user.repository.port.ts
    post/
      commands/
      queries/
      ports/
    shared/
      ports/
        email.port.ts
        event-bus.port.ts
        cache.port.ts

  infrastructure/            # External integrations
    persistence/
      prisma/
        prisma.service.ts
        repositories/
          prisma-user.repository.ts
          prisma-post.repository.ts
        migrations/
        schema.prisma
    messaging/
      redis-event-bus.ts
      bullmq-job-processor.ts
    external-services/
      stripe.adapter.ts
      sendgrid.adapter.ts
      s3.adapter.ts
    cache/
      redis-cache.adapter.ts

  presentation/              # API layer
    rest/
      controllers/
        user.controller.ts
        post.controller.ts
      middleware/
      dto/
    graphql/
      resolvers/
        user.resolver.ts
        post.resolver.ts
      schema.graphql
    grpc/
      user.grpc.controller.ts

  config/
  main.ts
```

## Shared Module Patterns

### Utility Functions

```typescript
// shared/utils/pagination.ts
export interface PaginationOptions {
  page: number;
  limit: number;
}

export interface PaginatedResult<T> {
  data: T[];
  meta: {
    page: number;
    limit: number;
    total: number;
    totalPages: number;
    hasNext: boolean;
    hasPrev: boolean;
  };
}

export function paginate<T>(
  items: T[],
  total: number,
  options: PaginationOptions
): PaginatedResult<T> {
  const totalPages = Math.ceil(total / options.limit);
  return {
    data: items,
    meta: {
      page: options.page,
      limit: options.limit,
      total,
      totalPages,
      hasNext: options.page < totalPages,
      hasPrev: options.page > 1,
    },
  };
}
```

### Barrel Exports

```typescript
// modules/user/index.ts
export { UserModule } from './user.module';
export { UserService } from './user.service';
export { User } from './user.entity';
export { CreateUserDto, UpdateUserDto } from './user.dto';

// Other modules import from the barrel:
import { UserService } from '../user';
```

## Configuration Management

```typescript
// config/app.config.ts
import { z } from 'zod';

const envSchema = z.object({
  NODE_ENV: z.enum(['development', 'staging', 'production']).default('development'),
  PORT: z.coerce.number().default(3000),
  DATABASE_URL: z.string().url(),
  REDIS_URL: z.string().url(),
  JWT_SECRET: z.string().min(32),
  JWT_EXPIRATION: z.string().default('15m'),
  CORS_ORIGINS: z.string().transform(s => s.split(',')),
  LOG_LEVEL: z.enum(['debug', 'info', 'warn', 'error']).default('info'),
});

export type AppConfig = z.infer<typeof envSchema>;

export function loadConfig(): AppConfig {
  const result = envSchema.safeParse(process.env);
  if (!result.success) {
    console.error('Invalid environment configuration:');
    console.error(result.error.format());
    process.exit(1);
  }
  return result.data;
}
```

## Monorepo Structure

For larger projects with multiple services:

```
packages/
  shared/                    # Shared types, utils, errors
    src/
      types/
      utils/
      errors/
    package.json
  database/                  # Shared Prisma schema
    prisma/
      schema.prisma
    src/
      client.ts
    package.json
  api-gateway/               # Public API
    src/
    package.json
  user-service/              # Microservice
    src/
    package.json
  order-service/             # Microservice
    src/
    package.json
  notification-service/      # Microservice
    src/
    package.json
package.json                 # Root workspace config
tsconfig.base.json           # Shared TypeScript config
turbo.json                   # Or nx.json for monorepo tooling
```

## Module Dependency Rules

```
+--------+     +--------+     +--------+
| Order  |---->| User   |     | Post   |
| Module |     | Module |<----| Module |
+--------+     +--------+     +--------+
    |               |               |
    v               v               v
+----------------------------------------+
|           Shared Module                |
| (database, auth, utils, errors)        |
+----------------------------------------+

Rules:
1. Modules can depend on Shared
2. Modules can depend on other modules' exported services
3. No circular dependencies between modules
4. Shared depends on nothing application-specific
```

## Key Principles

1. **Colocation**: Keep related files together (tests next to code)
2. **Feature isolation**: Each module is self-contained
3. **Clear boundaries**: Modules expose explicit public APIs via barrel exports
4. **Dependency direction**: Dependencies flow toward the domain core
5. **Configuration validation**: Validate env vars at startup, not at runtime
6. **Consistent naming**: Use a naming convention and stick to it
7. **Index files for public API**: Each module has an index.ts that exports only what other modules need
5.7.3.intuition: Structuring a large project is like organizing a city. The anti-pattern is like putting all restaurants on one street, all houses on another, and all offices on a third -- everyone has to commute everywhere. Feature-based structure is like neighborhoods where each one has its own shops, homes, and offices. People mostly stay in their neighborhood (module) and only travel to others when necessary.

exercises.title: Restructure a Flat Project
exercises.description: Take a project with everything in flat folders (controllers/, services/, models/) and restructure it into feature-based modules. Each module should be self-contained with its own controller, service, repository, DTOs, and tests. Create barrel exports for each module.
exercises.hint: Create a modules/ directory. Move each feature's files into its own folder. Add index.ts barrel exports. Update import paths.

exercises.title: Validate Configuration at Startup
exercises.description: Create a configuration module using Zod that validates all required environment variables at application startup. If any are missing or invalid, the app should print a clear error message and exit immediately.
exercises.hint: Use z.object() with all env vars. Call envSchema.safeParse(process.env) in a loadConfig() function called from main.ts.

exercises.title: Enforce Module Boundaries
exercises.description: Set up ESLint rules (or TypeScript project references) that prevent circular dependencies between modules. Module A should not be able to import from Module B's internal files -- only from its barrel export.
exercises.hint: Use eslint-plugin-import with no-restricted-imports rule, or use TypeScript project references with composite: true.

exercises.title: Create a Monorepo Template
exercises.description: Set up a monorepo using npm workspaces (or pnpm/yarn workspaces) with three packages: shared (types and utils), api (Express or NestJS app), and worker (background job processor). The api and worker packages should import from shared.
exercises.hint: Use 'workspaces' in root package.json. Reference shared package by name in api and worker dependencies.

problems.problem: Circular dependencies between modules
problems.cause: Module A imports from Module B, and Module B imports from Module A.
problems.solution: Extract the shared dependency into a third module or use events for loose coupling. If UserModule and OrderModule both need each other, extract shared types into a Shared module.

problems.problem: The shared/ folder becomes a dumping ground
problems.cause: Developers put anything that does not clearly belong to a module into shared/.
problems.solution: Be strict about what goes in shared/: only truly cross-cutting concerns (auth, logging, database, error handling). If something is used by only 2 modules, consider keeping it in one and importing from the other.

problems.problem: Import paths are extremely long and fragile
problems.cause: Deep nesting results in imports like '../../../shared/utils/pagination.ts'.
problems.solution: Configure TypeScript path aliases in tsconfig.json: { "paths": { "@shared/*": ["src/shared/*"], "@modules/*": ["src/modules/*"] } }. Use these aliases in imports.
